topic: How Binary Code Controls Everything Digital
title: Why 0s and 1s Run the Digital World

lesson.learn:
Binary code works because computers can reliably tell two states apart, such as on and off or high and low voltage. Those states are mapped to 0 and 1, and from that simple idea come text, images, music, software, and the internet itself. The whole digital world rests on that two-state logic.

The elegance of binary is that it turns physical signals into information. Once a machine can distinguish two states quickly and accurately, it can build anything more complex from that foundation.
lesson.simpler: Computers use 0s and 1s because circuits are good at choosing between two states. Bigger digital systems are built from that simple idea.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Binary is the basic language that turns electrical states into digital information.
lesson.deeperDive:
A bit is a single binary digit, and groups of bits form bytes, instructions, and data formats. Logic gates combine bits into decisions, and transistors make those gates possible at speed and scale. The same basic structure can represent numbers, letters, sounds, and images through standardized encodings.

That is why binary is both extremely simple and extraordinarily powerful. It is the smallest possible digital alphabet, but it can describe almost anything once enough bits are arranged the right way.
lesson.funFact: One byte usually contains eight bits.
references: IBM - binary numbers and digital logic

people: George Boole - created the logic behind binary decisions
Claude Shannon - linked logic to digital circuits
John von Neumann - shaped computer architecture
Grace Hopper - influenced programming and machine-level thinking
places: Bell Labs - logic and transistor research
Computer hardware factories - where binary becomes machines
concepts: Logic gate - a building block of digital decisions
Transistor - the switch that makes binary hardware possible
Machine code - instructions written in the form computers execute directly
